title: FinitePart
summary: Defines the finite part as the constant Laurent coefficient and the log anomaly as the logarithmic coefficient.

mathematicalContent.heading: Mathematical role
mathematicalContent.body: Defines the finite part as the constant Laurent coefficient and the log anomaly as the logarithmic coefficient.

mathematicalContent.heading: Formal boundary
mathematicalContent.body: This page describes exactly the declarations present in Ambient/PE/Renormalization/FinitePart.lean; claims not represented by a declaration are not counted as verified.

correspondence.mathematicalMeaning: Selects or identifies the constant term in a Laurent/log cutoff expansion.
correspondence.leanType: def finitePart {R : Type u} [Zero R] (E : LaurentLogExpansion R) : R

correspondence.mathematicalMeaning: Selects or identifies the logarithmic coefficient in a cutoff expansion.
correspondence.leanType: def logAnomaly {R : Type u} [Zero R] (E : LaurentLogExpansion R) : R

correspondence.mathematicalMeaning: Definition: Has No Log Term. The exact mathematical type is preserved from the Lean signature shown alongside it.
correspondence.leanType: def HasNoLogTerm {R : Type u} [Zero R] (E : LaurentLogExpansion R) : Prop

correspondence.mathematicalMeaning: Selects or identifies the constant term in a Laurent/log cutoff expansion.
correspondence.leanType: theorem finitePart_eq_coeff_zero {R : Type u} [Zero R] (E : LaurentLogExpansion R) : finitePart E = E.coeff 0

correspondence.mathematicalMeaning: Selects or identifies the logarithmic coefficient in a cutoff expansion.
correspondence.leanType: theorem logAnomaly_eq_logCoeff {R : Type u} [Zero R] (E : LaurentLogExpansion R) : logAnomaly E = E.logCoeff

references.locator: Proposition 3.7, Proposition 3.10, Lemma 4.1, and Theorems 1.1/1.4 as applicable
references.note: Primary source for the renormalized-curvature-integral mechanism represented by these modules.

limitations: This file establishes syntax, aggregate imports, or an abstract bridge. It must not be read as a derivation of the corresponding geometric theorem from concrete tensor and analytic data.
